title: Neural Turing Machines & Differentiable Neural Computers
subtitle: When Neural Networks Got Their Own RAM

children.children: What if a neural network had a scratch pad?

children: LSTMs have a few hundred numbers of hidden state. Transformers have attention over past tokens but no explicit workspace. In 2014, Alex Graves asked a radical question: what if we gave a neural network an actual memory bank — a matrix it could read from and write to, using learned addressing? The result was the

children.children: Neural Turing Machine

children: (NTM), and its successor, the

children.children: Differentiable Neural Computer

children: (DNC). These architectures didn't just process sequences — they

children.children: computed

children: with structured memory. They learned to copy, sort, and traverse graphs. And then the field moved on to transformers. This tutorial tells their story — what they got right, why they didn't scale, and why their ideas may be due for a comeback.

children.children: Before NTMs, recurrent neural networks had a fundamental limitation: their entire memory was their hidden state vector. An LSTM with 512 hidden units has exactly 512 numbers to store everything it has ever seen. This is like trying to write a novel using only a sticky note.

Consider what an LSTM must pack into its hidden state when processing the sentence: "The patient who was referred by Dr. Smith, who graduated from Harvard Medical School in 1995 and completed a residency at Johns Hopkins, arrived at the clinic."
The LSTM must simultaneously encode:
Who is the main subject (the patient)
Who referred them (Dr. Smith)
Where Dr. Smith went to school (Harvard)
When (1995)
Where the residency was (Johns Hopkins)
That we're still waiting for the main verb

children: All of this in, say, 512 floating-point numbers. And every new token

children.children: overwrites

children: parts of this state. The LSTM's gating mechanism tries to protect important information, but it's fundamentally playing Tetris — cramming more and more facts into a fixed-size space.

children.children: The insight behind NTMs was simple and profound: separate the computation from the storage. Give the network a controller (the 'CPU') and a memory bank (the 'RAM'), and let it learn to use the memory.

props.title: 2. The Neural Turing Machine (Graves et al., 2014)

children.children: A Turing machine has three components: a tape (infinite memory), a head (reads and writes to tape), and a controller (decides what to do). The Neural Turing Machine mirrors this with differentiable components that can be trained end-to-end via backpropagation.

props.concept: NTM Architecture — A Librarian and Their Library

children: Think of the NTM as a

children.children: librarian

children: (the controller network) managing a

children.children: library

children: (the memory matrix). The librarian can:

children.children: Read:

children: Look up books by content ("find me something about cats") or by location ("what's on shelf 3?")

children.children: Write:

children: Add new books, erase old ones, or update existing entries

children.children: Navigate:

children: Move to the next shelf (sequential access) or jump to a specific shelf (random access)

children.children: The magic: the librarian learns which books to read, what to write, and where to look — all from training data, with no explicit programming.

The memory is a simple 2D matrix M of size N × W, where N is the number of memory locations (slots) and W is the width of each slot (a vector). Think of it as N rows, each storing a W-dimensional vector.
A memory with N=4 slots and W=3 width:

Slot 0: [0.2, 0.8, 0.1]   ← maybe stores "cat"
Slot 1: [0.9, 0.1, 0.3]   ← maybe stores "chased"
Slot 2: [0.3, 0.7, 0.5]   ← maybe stores "dog"
Slot 3: [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]   ← empty (available)
Real NTMs use something like N=128 slots × W=20 width. The model decides what each slot means through training.

children: Reading from memory uses a

children.children: weight vector

children: w of length N, where each entry w_i says "how much attention to pay to slot i." The read result is a weighted sum:
This is

children.children: exactly

children: the same mechanism as attention in transformers — a weighted combination of value vectors. In fact, transformers can be seen as a simplified version of this: every token's representation is a 'memory slot,' and attention weights determine which slots to read. The NTM just made the memory explicit and separate from the input.

children.children: Writing is a two-step process. First, selectively erase old content; then, add new content:

Step 1 — Erase:
  M[i] ← M[i] · (1 - w_i · e)     where e is the "erase vector"
  (w_i=1, e=1 → slot fully erased)
  (w_i=0 → slot untouched)

Step 2 — Add:
  M[i] ← M[i] + w_i · a            where a is the "add vector"
  (writes new content proportional to attention weight)

The controller network produces both the erase vector e and the add vector a. This gives the model fine-grained control: it can overwrite a slot completely (erase then add), append information (add without erase), or partially modify content.

props.title: 2.4 Addressing: How to Find the Right Slot

children.children: This is where NTMs get clever. The model can find memory locations in two fundamentally different ways, and can blend them:
Content-based addressing

children: — "Find the slot that looks like this key vector."

children.children:
For each slot i:
  similarity(i) = cosine(k, M[i])        k = key vector from controller
  w_content(i) = softmax(β · similarity)  β = sharpness parameter

High β → sharp focus on best match
Low β  → blurry attention over similar slots
Location-based addressing

children: — "Shift attention from where I was to a nearby slot."

children.children:
Three steps:
1. Interpolation gate g ∈ [0,1]:
   w_gated = g · w_content + (1-g) · w_previous
   (g=1: use content lookup; g=0: keep previous location)

2. Convolutional shift s:
   w_shifted(i) = Σ_j w_gated(j) · s(i-j)
   (s = [0.1, 0.8, 0.1] means "mostly stay, slight chance of ±1")

3. Sharpening γ ≥ 1:
   w_final(i) = w_shifted(i)^γ / Σ_j w_shifted(j)^γ
   (prevents attention from blurring over repeated shifts)

children: This combination is remarkably powerful. The model can look up a value by content, then sequentially scan forward from that location. It's the difference between "find 'John' in the phonebook" (content) and "read the next three entries" (location-based shift)

props.title: Q: How would you use those 3 addressing tools to do this?

children.children: To "read the next three entries" after finding "John," the NTM chains all three addressing tools in sequence. First, content-based addressing scans every memory location and compares it against a key vector representing "John." This produces a weight distribution heavily focused on, say, location 42 where John's entry lives. The interpolation gate then decides how much to trust this fresh content lookup versus where the head was already pointing. Since we just did a deliberate lookup, the gate would be set high, mostly adopting the new content-based weights centered on location 42.

Now the location-based shift takes over. The shift distribution acts like a small convolutional kernel over the weight vector. To move one entry forward, the model emits a shift weighted toward +1. After reading location 42, shifting by +1 moves focus to location 43. On the next time step, the model can suppress the content-based lookup by setting the interpolation gate low, keeping focus near where it already is, and shifting by +1 again to reach location 44. One more step gets it to location 45. Across three consecutive time steps, it reads locations 43, 44, and 45, the three entries after John.

The key insight is that content-based addressing fires once to establish a starting point, then the interpolation gate effectively "turns off" content lookup on subsequent steps so the head does not jump somewhere else. The shift mechanism then does the sequential scanning. Sharpening also matters here because you want the head crisply focused on one location at a time rather than blurring across neighbors. A high sharpening parameter keeps the weights peaked so each read pulls from exactly one entry rather than a smeared average of several.

Think of it like using your finger in a real phonebook. You scan the page looking for "John" (content addressing), you plant your finger there (interpolation commits to that spot), then you drag your finger down line by line (shift), keeping it pressed firmly so you read one entry at a time, not a blur of ink (sharpening).
Asked about ""read the next three entries" ..." — 3/20/2026, 3:29:12 PM
NTMs demonstrated something remarkable: they could learn algorithms from input-output examples alone, with no explicit programming.

Given a sequence of vectors, output the same sequence. Trivial for a human, but requires the model to: (1) write each input to sequential memory slots, (2) after seeing the end-of-input marker, rewind to the beginning, (3) read and output each slot in order.

Input:  [A, B, C, D, END]
Output: [A, B, C, D]

Learned behavior:
  Step 1-4: Write A→slot0, B→slot1, C→slot2, D→slot3
  Step 5:   Detect END, shift read head to slot 0
  Step 6-9: Read slot0→A, shift+1, read slot1→B, ...

The NTM learned to use memory as a tape — writing sequentially, then reading sequentially. An LSTM can also learn copy, but its performance degrades sharply for sequences longer than those seen in training. The NTM generalized: trained on length-20 sequences, it could copy length-100 sequences perfectly, because the algorithm it learned (write sequentially, read sequentially) doesn't depend on length.

Given (key, value) pairs, output the values sorted by key. The NTM learned a form of content-based retrieval — scanning memory for the minimum key, outputting its value, marking it as used, and repeating. This isn't bubble sort or merge sort; it's a neural network inventing its own sorting procedure using the primitives of read, write, and address.

Given pairs like (A→X, B→Y, C→Z), then queried with B, output Y. This requires content-based addressing: look up the slot where B is stored, then read the associated value. This is key-value memory — the same concept that powers transformer attention, but discovered here first as an explicit mechanism.

The profound result wasn't that NTMs solved these tasks — purpose-built algorithms solve them trivially. It was that the NTM discovered algorithmic strategies from data alone. Nobody told it to use sequential addressing for copying. Nobody told it to use content-based lookup for association. The architecture provided the computational primitives (read, write, address by content, shift by location), and gradient descent found the right way to compose them.

children: This is the core argument for providing models with

children.children: computational structure without hardcoding knowledge

children: . The NTM doesn't know what sorting is. It has the tools to invent a sorting procedure, and training pressure to do so. This distinction — between providing primitives and providing solutions — is the key insight that may still be underexploited in modern architectures.

props.title: 4. The Differentiable Neural Computer (Graves et al., 2016)

children: Two years later, Graves and colleagues at DeepMind published the DNC — a more sophisticated version that solved several of the NTM's limitations. Published in

children.children: Nature

children: , it demonstrated graph traversal, question answering, and multi-step reasoning from examples.

props.concept: NTM vs DNC — Notepad vs Filing Cabinet

children: The NTM is like a

children.children: notepad

children: — you write things down in order and flip through pages. The DNC is like a

children.children: filing cabinet with an index system
It knows which drawers are empty (dynamic memory allocation)
It tracks the order things were filed (temporal linking)
It can free up old drawers when they're no longer needed (memory deallocation)
It has multiple clerks who can read simultaneously (multiple read heads)
The NTM had no concept of "which slots are free." It could accidentally overwrite important data. The DNC adds a usage vector u that tracks how much each slot has been used:

u ∈ [0,1]^N    (0 = totally free, 1 = heavily used)

Allocation weights (prefer free slots):
  Sort slots by usage: φ = argsort(u)   (least-used first)
  a(φ[j]) = (1 - u(φ[j])) · Π_{i=1}^{j-1} u(φ[i])

This means: allocate to the least-used slot,
but only if all even-less-used slots are already taken.

This is analogous to malloc() in C — the system finds free memory for you. The DNC can also deallocate: when a read head accesses a slot, its usage can be reduced via a "free gate," signaling that the data has been consumed and the slot can be reused.

This is the DNC's most elegant addition. It maintains an N × N matrix L where L[i,j] records "slot j was written immediately after slot i." This lets the model traverse memories in the order they were written — forward or backward.

After writing to slots in order: 3, 7, 1, 5

Temporal links:
  L[3,7] ≈ 1   (7 was written after 3)
  L[7,1] ≈ 1   (1 was written after 7)
  L[1,5] ≈ 1   (5 was written after 1)

Forward traversal from slot 3:
  f = L^T · w   →  follows the chain 3→7→1→5

Backward traversal from slot 5:
  b = L · w     →  follows the chain 5→1→7→3

children: Why does this matter? Because it separates

children.children: temporal order

children: from

children.children: physical location

children: . In an NTM, if you write things to slots 0, 1, 2, 3 sequentially, then the physical order matches the temporal order. But if memory gets fragmented (you free slot 1, reuse it for something new), the physical layout no longer reflects the writing order. The temporal link matrix preserves this history regardless of where things are physically stored. It's a linked list implemented as a matrix.

children.children: The DNC uses multiple independent read heads, each with its own addressing mechanism. Each head can independently choose between three addressing modes:
Content lookup:

children: Find slots matching a key (like the NTM)

children.children: Forward temporal:

children: Read what was written next after the current focus

children.children: Backward temporal:

children: Read what was written just before the current focus

children.children: A learned "read mode" vector π ∈ ℝ³ blends these three modes per head. This lets one head do content lookup ("find the node labeled 'London'") while another follows temporal links ("what was stored right after that?").
The DNC's flagship result was learning to traverse graphs from examples. Given a description of a graph (edges presented as pairs) and a query ("find a path from A to E"), the DNC learned to:
Store each node and its connections in memory
Use content-based addressing to find the start node
Follow temporal and content links to traverse edges
Output the path
It generalized to graph structures not seen in training — different sizes, different connectivities. The learned algorithm was genuinely general, not a memorized lookup table.

props.title: 5. NTM vs DNC: Side by Side

children.children:
Feature              │ NTM (2014)           │ DNC (2016)
─────────────────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────
Memory               │ N×W matrix           │ N×W matrix
Read heads           │ 1                    │ Multiple (typically 4)
Write heads          │ 1                    │ 1
Content addressing   │ ✓ (cosine + softmax) │ ✓ (cosine + softmax)
Location addressing  │ Shift + interpolation│ Temporal links (L matrix)
Memory allocation    │ None (overwrite risk)│ Usage tracking + free gates
Deallocation         │ None                 │ ✓ (free gates)
Sequential access    │ Convolutional shift  │ Temporal link traversal
Controller           │ LSTM or feedforward  │ LSTM (typically)
Published in         │ arXiv                │ Nature
Despite their elegance, NTMs and DNCs never achieved the dominance of transformers. Understanding why is as instructive as understanding how they work.

Every memory operation uses soft attention over ALL N slots. Reading costs O(N·W) and writing costs the same. With multiple read heads and the temporal link matrix (N×N), the DNC's memory overhead scales quadratically with the number of memory slots.

Per-timestep cost:
  NTM:  O(N·W)           for read + write
  DNC:  O(R·N·W + N²)    for R read heads + temporal links
  LSTM: O(H²)             for hidden size H

With N=256 slots, W=64, R=4 read heads:
  DNC per step: ~65K + 65K = ~130K operations
  LSTM per step: ~262K operations (H=512)

Seems comparable, but the DNC operations are harder to
parallelize and have complex gradient flows.

The real issue: increasing memory capacity requires increasing N, which makes the temporal link matrix grow as N². A DNC with 10,000 memory slots needs a 100-million-element link matrix. This doesn't scale the way simply adding transformer layers or widening hidden dimensions does.

The soft addressing mechanism creates extremely complex gradient landscapes. The content-based lookup involves cosine similarity inside a softmax inside a gated interpolation — gradients have to flow through all of this. Small changes in the key vector can cause the attention to jump discontinuously between slots, making optimization fragile.

Transformers, by contrast, have a much more direct path: Q·K^T is a simple dot product, and the gradient flows cleanly through the softmax. The attention mechanism is essentially the same (content-based soft lookup), but stripped of the location-based addressing, interpolation gates, and sharpening steps that make NTM training finicky.

children: In 2017, "Attention Is All You Need" showed that you don't need recurrence at all — just attention over the input sequence, parallelized across all positions simultaneously. This was the NTM's content-based addressing,

children.children: simplified and scaled

NTM:         Controller → produces key → content lookup over memory
Transformer: Each token → produces Q,K,V → attention over all tokens

Key simplifications:
  ✗ No separate memory bank (tokens ARE memory)
  ✗ No location-based addressing
  ✗ No write mechanism (memory is read-only within a layer)
  ✗ No usage tracking or allocation
  ✓ Massively parallel (no sequential dependency)
  ✓ Simple gradient flow
  ✓ Scales to billions of parameters

Transformers can be viewed as NTMs that traded explicit memory management for simplicity and parallelism. They gave up the ability to write, allocate, and deallocate memory — and it turned out that for most tasks, you don't need those things if you have enough parameters and enough attention heads.
But the simplification has costs:
No true write:

children: Transformers can't selectively store and later retrieve a fact. They must encode everything into the residual stream, which is overwritten by subsequent layers. (NTMs could explicitly write to a slot and read it back later.)

children.children: No memory management:

children: Every token gets the same amount of 'memory' (one slot in the KV cache). There's no way to allocate more space for important information or free up space when something becomes irrelevant.

children.children: No temporal linking:

children: Transformers use positional encoding to track order, but can't follow chains of associations the way DNCs traverse their temporal link matrix.

children.children: Context window limits:

children: A transformer's 'memory' is its context window. Everything beyond that window is simply gone. NTMs/DNCs had a fixed-size memory that could, in principle, compress and manage information indefinitely.

children.children: These are exactly the capabilities that modern LLM research is now trying to bolt back on: retrieval-augmented generation (external memory), chain-of-thought (explicit workspace), context extension (bigger memory). The NTM had all of these as built-in architectural features.

props.title: 7. The Legacy: Ideas That Outlived the Architecture

children.children: NTMs and DNCs may not have scaled, but their ideas are everywhere in modern deep learning — often unacknowledged.

children: The NTM's content-based addressing IS attention. The paper predates "Attention Is All You Need" by three years. When Bahdanau et al. (2015) introduced attention for machine translation, they cited the NTM. The transformer took this one idea — soft lookup by content similarity — and made it the

children.children: entire

children: architecture.

children.children: The idea of external memory lives on in: Memory Networks (Weston et al., 2014), End-to-End Memory Networks (Sukhbaatar et al., 2015), Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and even tool use in modern LLMs (where the 'tool' is an external memory system). Every time a model looks something up in a database, it's doing a simplified version of what NTMs did.

The NTM showed that traditional data structures (arrays, stacks, queues) could be made differentiable and trained with gradient descent. This spawned a line of research on differentiable stacks (Grefenstette et al., 2015), differentiable queues, and differentiable program interpreters. The principle: don't hard-code the algorithm, provide computational primitives and let the network learn how to compose them.

Perhaps the most important legacy: the separation of computation (controller) from storage (memory). This is the von Neumann architecture applied to neural networks. Modern state space models (Mamba, S4) have a version of this — the hidden state is a persistent memory that the input-dependent gates (the 'controller') read from and write to. The key difference is that SSMs use a flat state vector rather than an addressable memory bank, but the principle of separating processing from storage is the same.

props.title: 8. Connection to Stack-SSM: The Next Chapter?

children.children: The NTM/DNC story poses a tantalizing question: what if they had the right idea but the wrong substrate?

NTMs tried to give neural networks general-purpose memory with arbitrary read/write/address operations. This was too much — too many degrees of freedom, too complex to optimize, too expensive to scale. Transformers went the other direction: strip away explicit memory, use the input sequence itself as memory, and just scale.

children: But maybe there's a middle path. State space models like Mamba already have persistent state (the hidden vector) that's separate from the input. What if you gave that state

children.children: just enough

children: structure — not a full addressable memory bank, but something like a stack? Not arbitrary read/write, but push and pop?

children.children:
Memory complexity spectrum:

  LSTM          Mamba         Stack-SSM       NTM           DNC
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  flat vector   flat vector   stack of        N×W matrix    N×W matrix
  (h ∈ ℝ^H)    (h ∈ ℝ^{D×N}) vectors         + addressing  + links
                              (push/pop)                    + allocation
  
  ← less structure                              more structure →
  ← easier to train                             harder to train →
  ← less expressive memory                      more expressive memory →

children: Stack-SSM sits in the sweet spot: more structured than flat state (can handle nesting, scope, recursion), but simpler than full addressable memory (only two operations: push and pop). The NTM taught us that providing computational primitives to neural networks is powerful. The lesson from its failure is:

children.children: choose the right primitives for the problem

children: . For language, push and pop may be the right primitives — because language is fundamentally about nested scope, not arbitrary random access.

children.children: The deepest lesson from this line of work isn't about any specific architecture. It's about the relationship between structure and learning:
Too much structure

children: (NTM/DNC): Hard to train, doesn't scale, but learns genuine algorithms

children.children: Too little structure

children: (Transformer): Easy to train, scales beautifully, but wastes parameters re-discovering structure via superposition

children.children: Just right?

children: (Stack-SSM, or its successors): Provide the right computational primitives, let the model learn how to use them

children.children: The ultimate goal might be a model that doesn't just use hand-designed primitives like push/pop, but discovers its own. The NTM's general-purpose memory was an attempt at this, but the search space was too large. A more constrained starting point — with primitives that match the structure of the target domain — might give gradient descent enough of a foothold to discover operations we haven't thought of.

2014 │ Neural Turing Machines            │ Graves et al.     │ arXiv
     │ (content + location addressing,    │                   │
     │  differentiable read/write)        │                   │
─────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┤
2015 │ Memory Networks                    │ Weston et al.     │ ICLR
     │ Learning to Transduce (diff. stack)│ Grefenstette et al│ NIPS
     │ Attention for MT (Bahdanau)        │ Bahdanau et al.   │ ICLR
─────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┤
2016 │ Differentiable Neural Computer     │ Graves et al.     │ Nature
     │ (temporal links, allocation,       │                   │
     │  multiple read heads, free gates)  │                   │
─────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┤
2017 │ Attention Is All You Need          │ Vaswani et al.    │ NIPS
     │ (NTM content addressing, massively │                   │
     │  simplified and parallelized)      │                   │
─────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┤
2018 │ Relational Memory Core             │ Santoro et al.    │ NIPS
     │ Ordered Neurons (ON-LSTM)          │ Shen et al.       │ ICLR
─────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┤
2023 │ Mamba (selective SSMs)             │ Gu & Dao          │ arXiv
     │ (input-dependent state = learned   │                   │
     │  controller-memory interaction)    │                   │

questions.question: What is the key difference between how an NTM and an LSTM store information?
options: NTMs use attention; LSTMs use gates
NTMs have an external memory matrix separate from the controller; LSTMs store everything in hidden state
NTMs are recurrent; LSTMs are feedforward
NTMs use discrete memory; LSTMs use continuous memory
questions.explanation: The NTM's core innovation is separating computation (the controller) from storage (the memory matrix). An LSTM must pack everything into its fixed-size hidden state vector — there's no separate workspace. The NTM's controller can read from and write to an external memory bank.

questions.question: What does the DNC's temporal link matrix track?
options: How similar two memory slots are
How often each slot has been read
The order in which memory slots were written to
How long ago each slot was last accessed
questions.explanation: The temporal link matrix L records which slot was written immediately after which other slot, creating a linked list of write order. This lets the DNC follow temporal chains forward ('what was written next?') or backward ('what was written before?'), independent of physical memory location.

questions.question: Why is the NTM's content-based addressing significant for the history of deep learning?
options: It was the first use of cosine similarity in neural networks
It directly inspired the attention mechanism used in transformers
It proved that neural networks are Turing-complete
It replaced backpropagation with reinforcement learning
questions.explanation: The NTM's content-based addressing (compute similarity between a query and memory slots, then softmax to get weights, then weighted sum) is exactly the attention mechanism. Bahdanau et al. cited the NTM when introducing attention for machine translation in 2015, and the transformer's self-attention is a parallelized version of this same operation.

questions.question: What's the main reason NTMs/DNCs didn't scale as well as transformers?
options: They require labeled data
Soft attention over all memory slots plus complex addressing creates hard-to-optimize, expensive computation
They can only process fixed-length sequences
They need quantum computers
questions.explanation: NTMs/DNCs have complex gradient flows (cosine similarity inside softmax inside interpolation gates), quadratic scaling of the temporal link matrix (N²), and sequential processing that prevents parallelization. Transformers simplified to just the content-based attention part, enabling massive parallelism and cleaner gradients.

questions.question: Where does Stack-SSM sit on the structure spectrum between LSTMs and DNCs?
options: Less structure than an LSTM
Same as a DNC but faster
Between flat state (LSTM/Mamba) and full addressable memory (NTM/DNC) — structured but constrained
It replaces all memory with attention
questions.explanation: Stack-SSM adds just enough structure (push/pop operations on a stack of state frames) to handle nested scope without the full complexity of arbitrary read/write/address. It's more structured than Mamba's flat state but simpler than the NTM's general-purpose memory — targeting the sweet spot where the computational primitives match the structure of language.
